speaker: interviewer
text: '95, tape two. Mr. Hirsch, I'd like to begin by talking about your earliest school, the first school you went to, where that was located, and then move on from there. What was the first school and how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, start with six. I did not go to any religious preschool, neither one of us. And that was the grade school, five grades. And we had a wonderful teacher, who was my role model. He was very kind and gentle with all us-- a male teacher-- and I fondly remember him. And from there, I went into the gymnasium, which is eight years. So that gives you 13 years up to now. It was changed later. Grade school was only four years, and gymnasium was seven years. But during the period that I went to school, it was still the old order. Interestingly enough, the grade school and the gymnasium were just a few blocks down the street. So I could sleep late and still be on time. And the gymnasium was right adjacent to the grade school. And so it was very easy to get adjusted to the higher education. And eight years in gymnasium, where we had very intensive studying. If I compare it with my grandchildren, I realize that the study was much more intensive. I mean, I had eight years of Latin, six years of French, two years of English. We had-- actually what it amounts to is that the final two years I could have gone into a junior college here. That's how far you get. And it's all government schools. No tuition. There are a few private schools, predominantly for girls. There were no girls in our school until the seven and eight grade of gymnasium. That's when the problems started. And you could enter university if your grades were good. Not everybody had to enter university in those days. And your matura, which was the final exam at the end of the gymnasium, entitled you to go to university if you had good grades. The education system at the time was such that parents would already decide at the age of 10 or 11, whether the child would study-- the boy in those days-- would study or not. Because it was at that time that he would either go to a gymnasium or go to a [? Berufsschule, ?] which really means citizen school, which prepared the lad for a trade. And so you can see how stratified both the education and society was, because it was up to the intellectual level of the parents to groom the child to pursue the higher education.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were growing up, were you motivated to become an engineer? Did you always want to be an engineer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents tried their best to have me study medicine. They went so far as to send me to an uncle, who was a doctor, for four summers in a row, and also to a cousin, who was a doctor. And I, somehow-- it didn't-- to use a colloquialism-- turn me on. And I just felt engineering was my calling. And I pretty much also in my-- in the school, I particularly excelled in the realistic subjects, rather than in Latin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the technical university you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the time that I attended it, it was called Vienna Technische Hochschule, which literally translated means Viennese Technical High School. Hochschule has a different connotation in Europe than over here. It's a school of higher learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Typically after the war, the Austrians changed their name to Viennese Technical University. In order to overcome this, well, I wouldn't call it stigma, because after a year or two when you're out in the field, the school doesn't count anymore. It's what you're doing that counts. That goes for magnet schools as well. But throughout the world, if somebody had a diploma from a high school, what kind of an engineer was he? And so they changed it for that reason. Where was the school located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was located at the border between the first and the ninth district, and we walked to school. My friend and I, we met at a certain street and walked to school four times a day, because the big meal in Vienna was at noon. So we would go to school in the morning, come back at noon, have the big meal with the family, go back to school, and come back later in the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it? How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a half hour walk each way. It was a very pleasant walk through the middle of town, with all the most beautiful shopping streets. It was really a pleasure, I must say. And the conversation was flowing between the boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want you to know again, that this fellow, Ludwig Prater, whom I mentioned before, walked with us. It wasn't a question of Jews walking together, et cetera. It was different then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to school from '33, '34, till '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you complete the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did my first government exam with honors after two and a half years. I never got to the second government exam. Circumstances prevented that. I got special permission-- sorry I didn't bring this matriculation book with me, with a huge swastika in it. I got special permission to continue my studies. But as things developed and Dachau came in between, I did not finish my last exam. However, I did get what is called an absolutorium, which means a certificate that I completed those studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to continue your studies? You're saying it was special. I-- I take it because you were a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to get that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was able to go to school and partake in the sessions. I had a special identification card that permitted me to do that under the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who permitted you to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Nazis came, I had to apply for this permission. And they stamped this huge book that I had with all the classes with a swastika that said 'continued studies permitted.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '38, when Hitler invaded-- marched into Austria. He didn't have to invade much. It was pretty much a cakewalk.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that-- you told me that after you-- you left the university, you were drafted. Can you tell me a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- during my studies, I was drafted. However, being a student, I was deferred. I'd actually volunteered being a gung-ho engineer for the tank corps, of all things. And then later, of course, I got a letter that my status with the military is voided because I'm a 100% Jew. So that was a savior. That was good. There's nothing wrong with that, thinking back now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to, for a moment, discuss the political climate in Vienna, particularly from the years of 1933 up to the Anschluss. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When was the first time you heard the name Adolf Hitler and-- and-- and learned of his policies of discrimination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Discrimination, you said, or extermination?

speaker: interviewer
text: Discrimination.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was in '33. But I want to backtrack a little bit. After the war, Vienna was torn by political strife. We had two opposing parties, which at the present time are working together and govern the country seemingly with success. But in those days, it was a tremendous political rift. We had the Socialists on one side and the Catholic conservative party on the other side. Both of them were training armies. The Socialists had the armed camp. And the Catholics had their what was called--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which war are you referring to now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Second.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the First World War. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, sorry. And it came to clashes repeatedly. The Socialists had-- the Socialist Mayor of Vienna, had built houses for workers. And those houses were turned into fortresses, and they were armed. And the rightists had their own armies, predominantly drawn from the provinces. You see, Vienna was a cosmopolitan city. But the outskirts, they're very conservative and very religious. And the local church hierarchy had tremendous influence on the people. And these groups clashed repeatedly. They had actual mini civil wars in Vienna, where they were shooting cannons at these fortresses that the working class had built under the guidance of the leadership of the Social Democrats. It was a particular problem, because the leaders of the Social Democrats in Vienna were Jews. And when Hitler invaded Austria, they fled. And it led to a tremendous backlash among Gentile workers, who said, you escaped. You're out there, and we are here. And they all turned National, Socialistic. When I was working-- it was obligatory for engineering students to work every summer for a month or two in a factory somewheres to gain practical experience. And I worked for the largest Austrian diesel engine manufacturers, truck diesels. And I had contact with workers. And they were-- were all very leftist, very. But it changed rapidly. Now I want to continue with what you asked me. Naturally, we read, and there was already radio. We heard Hitler's speeches. But being middle class Austrians, and my relatives in Czechoslovakia, never thought that such a thing could happen to us. It's an interesting phenomenon. You feel comfortable in your surroundings. You're physically comfortable. And you can't foreseen-- can't foresee this overnight, dramatic change that can take place in people and in your living situation. And so there was no thought of building ahead and emigrating, et cetera. I'll give you an example. One of my relatives in Czechoslovakia was extremely well off. They were in Gablonz, which is the center of artificial jewelry. And he had a factory, and he had 31 representatives throughout the world selling his goods. When he escaped to England penniless, he had 31 representatives and nothing. How he built himself up again is another story. But I just want to show you, in no way did this man send his money out to Switzerland, anticipating anything like that. Even after '33, thinking that-- and this was in the Sudetenland, where my father was born, where the German language was the language and where the German influence was very predominant-- and yet, this thought never entered our mind. It only started to enter our mind and everybody's mind, I'm venturing to say, when Austria was invaded. It was then all of a sudden that we got shaken up. Because little by little, the terrorizing of the population took very subtle forms. They picked up Jews and had them wash the sidewalk-- older people. These type of things, little by little, made one realize that-- that-- that there's-- there's a different world coming along. And it was then that everybody scrambled to put their registration number down at the various consulates. We had relatives, my father and my mother, particularly my mother, had outstanding relatives in the United States. I will mention it later. When-- so we wrote to them for affidavits. And we registered, of course, with the United States consulate. But in those days, there was the quota system. The quota system was based upon the-- the numerical composition of the citizens of the United States. It was so many from Germany, and so few from Czechoslovakia, and so many from Italy. And according to that, a certain number were permitted to come here, and the reason was given according to availability. Now, Austria and Germany had a good quota. Czechoslovakia was terrible. And that was unfortunately the problem with my parents, because they were both born in what was at the time-- mind you, not what the monarchy was-- what was at the time Czechoslovakia. And as I say, as the climate changed, we took a whole different-- we started to take a different outlook.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year? Did you-- Did you-- would you say there was a collective--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not until '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe '37 we started to think about what's going on in Germany, et cetera. But the real thrust to do something came only after Hitler marched into Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I understand you correctly, you're saying that the entire Jewish population, in a sense, had lived in collective denial that they-- that this would not happen to them. This couldn't happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It can't happen here. Let me phrase it this way. And I think it could happen here, if you ask me. Here, meaning here.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will-- we will talk about that. But let me-- let me just interject one thing. The Nazis, however, did come to power-- you know, local Nazis, Austrian Nazis-- during the '30s. Presumably they--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. They had the same-- they had the same policies, did they not?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not come to-- openly to political power, because the regime was Catholic-influenced, particularly just before. There was the famous Dollfuss. There was Schuschnigg, who in his last radio speech, vehemently spoke against the Nazis and terminated by saying, not-- finished the speech by saying, God save Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, these people came out of the woodwork immediately upon the Anschluss. To give you an example, we had a mathematics teacher, Professor [? Hueba ?] in gymnasium, who was the most wonderful guy, really. And as soon as the Anschluss came, his brother became minister of justice [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under the new regime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how it went throughout. People from-- the amazing part is, the university people, who the average person would assume know more than the guy on the street, are the ones who embraced this completely. They were totally mesmerized.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mesmerized. They were totally mesmerized by this powerful speaker who was able, like any great leader-- let's be honest, Roosevelt was able to convince the people that he is the man of the century. In Hitler's case, the man of the millennium. He was going to-- to-- to make the Reich a 1,000-year Reich. But usually the man of the century. That-- that-- I'll be honest with you. That goes for any great leader. I don't care who he is. A good speaker, even he is otherwise weak, can take along the people. Reagan was an excellent speaker. I don't think he was a intellectual light, but he was an excellent speaker. Whether he learned his speeches or whether he memorized them is irrelevant. I'm sure every speaker memorizes and has a speech writer. And Hitler was a magnificent speaker. He was able to convince these people that this is the future of the German nation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any effective opposition amongst non-Jews to-- to the policies of the Nazis in the '30s before the Anschluss?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before the Anschluss, as I said before, they were fighting each other. The socialists and the rightists were continuously at loggerheads. The big city had a Socialist mayor. The Sudental provinces were very much conservative under the influence of the Catholic religion. And they just-- they were not in the mood to compromise. They didn't understand that compromise would save the country. And that made it also-- this cleavage made it easier also for them. And as soon as Hitler marched in, he-- he-- he didn't march into Vienna. Vienna is in the center. He marched through the provinces. And there he was greeted with great outpourings of sympathy. And by that time, by the time it came to Vienna, it was, as I said before, [NON-ENGLISH] was cakewalk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I must ask you about the role of the Catholic Church. You say that-- that Austria was a-- a-- a very Christian country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very Catholic country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the reaction of the Catholic Church to Hitler and his policies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, the Catholic Church basically was very conservative. And among the Catholics in Austria, there was really no sympathy for the Jews, because they were still teaching in the church that the Jews killed Christ and that the Jews would not accept that tenet. And so children were imbued with these thoughts and with the mother milk from the start. There's no question about that. And that continues to float up, even into the upper classes. And to the influence-- remember, if you go to Austria, you see this wonderful countryside. And every little town has this beautiful church. And you must remember, in that beautiful church, there must have been a delegate who was preaching the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And despite all the jokes that were made about the Austrian little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what should I call them? What would you call them? They're not important church people, but they are minor church officials-- run these little churches which are supported by the community. And all the jokes that were made about him and his teaching aide. It was a tremendous influence on Austrian thought. Tremendous, as far as I'm concerned.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was part of the-- of the problem in Austria, why Hitler was so readily accepted, because his-- his continuous going in vehemently against the Jewish conspiracies throughout the world, fitting very well with that mindset, even though he was the exact opposite of Catholicism. But it fit in with this mindset. There's no question about it. So there was no resistance to speak of. He was just rolling over the countryside.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to take you back to the date of March 12, 1938, when Hitler began his so-called bloodless invasion--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Steamrolled right in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a general strike in Vienna, I recall. We came out of university and I wanted to get home, so I ran and jumped onto the streetcar. And it was standing there. And the streetcar conductor said, what's your rush? We've been standing here for an hour. It's just some Jewish, a little humor. But generally speaking, it-- it really didn't take hold until these little by little terrorizing activities started to take place and the registration and the restrictions in school. And then the breaking down of Jewish stores and the stormtroopers marching in front and shouting, don't buy from the Jew. And gradually this kept increasing, increasing, where they started to take over Jewish businesses by force. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it escalated, you have to understand this. This is not like from this to here. It went like this. It escalated little by little, where in the end we realized that we are doomed. That's how it developed. It was not a-- a momentary thing-- on this day, from this day on. No. It escalated by terrorizing acts. They are the masters in that. I'll tell you later an episode, which will typify the mindset. But that is how it worked. We didn't have-- I want to explain something to you because we were talking about this before. In Czechoslovakia and in Austria, we didn't have pogroms. We didn't have Cossacks riding on horses through the towns with whips, which I personally have seen in my life when I visited my uncle. We didn't have anything like this. They had a very level life before '38-- before '33. Even after the monarchy broke up, there was no such thing. It was a different lifestyle.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say, however, that you actually witnessed a pogrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. That is a long story, but I'll squeeze it in. My mother's brother studied medicine in Vienna, and he lived with my father and mother while he was a student at the Viennese university. And he was drafted. And being a graduate physician, he was immediately made a lieutenant. That's the way it was. I presume it's similar here. I wouldn't know. And he went to war in the First World War, and he was injured at the Battle of the Fortress of [POLISH] which is Poland. You have to understand that under the monarchy, what later became Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Austria, northern Italy, a good part of northern Yugoslavia, Slovenia and Croatia, part of lower Poland-- Galacia-- part of Romania were all part of the Austrian monarchy. They were all imbued with this, shall I say, German spirit, in a way. When you go to Slovenia and Croatia and Yugoslavia, you will see all the big buildings painted yellow date from the Austrian monarchy. They have been taken over by Tito and made into old age homes, et cetera, and government offices. But it doesn't matter. There is very little new there, except magnificent hotels for tourism. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went into this battle in Poland, even though he was of Czech extraction. He was injured there and was nursed back to health by a Polish woman-- what is referred to in Polish szlachta, which means of noble blood. She was a Christian. And they fell in love, and they got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even though my grandfather was, I wouldn't call it reformed. There was no such thing in those days, but he was very progressive. He, if you know the very famous paintings, broke the sticks over his son. And he didn't rend his clothing, but he said, you're finished. And the whole family ostracized him for that. And he made a career there. He became what was referred to as the district physician, supervising government edicts and general health control, in addition to having his private practice. And the lady in question, with a mother who was a piano virtuoso, passed away. And here he was in this little town, in [INAUDIBLE] in Poland, with no relatives, nobody. All by himself. But he had kept up the friendship with my mother and father, because there he studied and there was a different bond. And so he wrote my mother to send him somebody to spend the summer with him. And there was this opportunity to get me to become a doctor. So she sent me there immediately. And speaking Czech, it took no time for me to learn Polish. I was already in engineering school there then. But there was a huge, the largest sawmill on the European continent there, owned by a-- actually a Viennese family, but run by a Hungarian family. They had changed their name from [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE]. And it was typical for Hungarian Jews to integrate and change their names. I don't know. That was a typical thing to pass. And so I was able to work there for six weeks each summer and spent the remaining six weeks with my uncle, traveling with him. He had a car and a chauffeur. Even though when it rains there, we had to use what is called droshky, which is a horse-drawn carriage. Because the mud is this high. No car can possibly negotiate that. I don't know how it is there today, but I have my suspicions in those little towns. And so I went with him to these various patients and administering his official duties as the district physician. And actually, I enjoyed being with him, because he was a very gentle, wonderful fellow, but it didn't take me away from engineering. And there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the little towns are mostly Jewish. And the farmers around it are, of course, Christians. And the joke went that in [INAUDIBLE], there are 50% heathen and 50% [? Yiddinen ?] That's what really means, 50% Jewish and 50% Gentiles. Anyway, the Jews had all little stores. There were-- there was the intelligentsia. There was the lawyer and the doctor and the pharmacist. And they would walk down Main Street every afternoon, and people would greet them. But the little storekeepers put their wares out. And the farmers would all come in on Saturday. And they would buy the clothes and everything. But then in the evening, they would get stone drunk. And then they would--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you to stop because this is a very important point. And we will go on with the next tape.
